sevenDayForecast.headline: Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle to cross Far North Queensland on Friday and eastern Northern Territory from late Saturday moving off WA coast next week.
points: Tropical Cyclone Advices and Forecast Track Maps are being issued and should be referred to for more detailed information
Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle is moving west and will cross over the Cape York Peninsula during Friday.

There is high confidence that it will continue to move west over the coming days continuing into the Gulf of Carpentaria, with a High chance it is a tropical cyclone as it impacts the eastern Northern Territory on the weekend.
Early next week Narelle is forecast to move offshore of the Kimberley coast where it has a High chance of re-developing into a tropical cyclone.

trackForecast.headline: Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle (34U) to move west over Queensland's Cape York Peninsula, across Gulf of Carpentaria to eastern NT coast Saturday night, and eventually re-intensify off the WA coast next Tuesday/Wednesday.
trackForecast.discussion:
Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle is an intense system off the Far North Queensland coast with a well-defined eye on satellite imagery.

The system will stay well north of Abbot Point with no threat of any direct wind impacts at the location.

There is high confidence in a sustained westerly track which means Narelle will:

Cross Cape York Peninsula Friday morning at a forecast category 5 intensity then weakening overland.

Redevelop in the Gulf of Carpentaria into a possible severe tropical cyclone again before crossing the NT coast late on Saturday. The current forecast track suggests it will pass close to Groote Eylandt on Saturday afternoon or evening, most likely as a category 3 system, with about a 25% risk that it will be a stronger category 4 system at this time.

Weakening over the NT on Sunday then reaching the Joseph Bonaparte on Monday. 

Moving off the Kimberley on Tuesday and reintensifying.
trackForecast.discussionUpperBound: The upper bound reflects the system developing faster than anticipated and remaining over water for longer.
